question: Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), armed modes are coloured:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), current data and values are coloured:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), selected data such as engaged autopilot modes are coloured:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), turbulence is coloured:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), a selected heading (heading bug) is coloured:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), the active route/flight plan is coloured:
What is the deviation per dot on the HSI when using an EFIS 2-dot RNAV system in the EN ROUTE (ENR) mode?
What is the deviation per dot on the HSI when using an EFIS 2-dot RNAV system in the ILS approach (APR) mode?
Which of the following lists all the stages of flight when it is possible to change the route in the active flight plan on an FMS equipped aircraft?
options: Only before the flight plan is activated
Only once the aircraft is airborne.
At any time before take-off and throughout the flight
true: At any time before take-off and throughout the flight

question: ICAO defines Area Navigation (RNAV) as a method of navigation which permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path:
options: Within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids or within the limits of the capability of self-contained aids, or a combination of these
Outside the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids provided that it is equipped with a minimum of one serviceable self-contained navigation aid
Outside the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids provided that it is equipped with a minimum of two serviceable self-contained navigation aids
Within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids provided that it is equipped with a minimum of one serviceable self-contained navigation aid
true: Within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids or within the limits of the capability of self-contained aids, or a combination of these

question: Which one of the following sensors/systems is self-contained?
Which of the following statements regarding B-RNAV is correct?
options: B-RNAV can be used up to FL95. Above FL95 P-RNAV is required.
B-RNAV is only applicable when flying in TMA's.
For 95% of the flight time, the track keeping accuracy must not exceed 5 NM.
In case of B-RNAV, RNP1 is required.
true: For 95% of the flight time, the track keeping accuracy must not exceed 5 NM.

question: Which statement about RNAV-routes is correct?
options: All waypoints of RNAV-routes are called phantom stations
RNAV-routes are only used in terminal areas in order to make more efficient use of the approach and landing facilities of an airport
In general RNAV-routes coincide with standard routes in order to make optimum use of the available VOR/DME-stations
A method of navigation which permIts aircraft operation on any desired flight path.
true: A method of navigation which permIts aircraft operation on any desired flight path.

question: A 2D RNAV system provides guidance in:
3D RNAV fixing gives you:
options: Horizontal, vertical profile and time guidance

question: A 3-dimensional RNAV system has capability in:
options: The horizontal plane and a speed management system.
The horizontal plane and in the vertical plane.
The horizontal plane and a cruise management system.
The horizontal plane, in the vertical plane and a timing function.
true: The horizontal plane and in the vertical plane.

question: A 2-dimensional RNAV system has a capability in the:
Basic RNAV requires a track-keeping accuracy of:
options: +/- 5NM or better for 95% of the flight time
+/- 3NM or better for 90% of the flight time
+/- 2NM or better for 75% of the flight time
true: +/- 5NM or better for 95% of the flight time

question:
Which of the following may be used for minimum RNAV performance:

1. NDB
2. IRS/INS
3. VOR/DME
4. GPS
Precision RNAV (P-RNAV) requires a track-keeping accuracy of:
options: ± 10.0 NM for 95% of the flight time.
± 1.0 NM for 95% of the flight time.
± 8.0 NM for 95% of the flight time.
± 5.0 NM for 95% of the flight time.
true: ± 1.0 NM for 95% of the flight time.

question:
The inputs of information used to achieve the RNAV required accuracy may be:

1. NDB
2. IRS
3. VOR/DME
4. GNSS The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:

Benefits of Area Navigation include:

1. Shorter flight distance
2. Reduction in fuel and flight time.
3. No radio contact within RNAV airspace.
4. Reduction in the number of ground training facilities
5. Pilot choice of vertical and horizontal separations. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
Which of the following Nav Aids will provide an RNAV system with position?

Area Navigation is a method of navigation that permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path within the limits of the capability of self contained systems. Which of the following systems is such a self contained sytem?
By which of the following flight deck equipment are the waypoints inserted into an  FMS RNAV-system by the pilot?
Which component of an Area Navigation System displays the Cross Track Distance?
One of the benefits of RNAV is:
options: RNAV allows to fly at RNAV flight level with a 500 ft separation.
RNAV allows aircraft to take a more direct flight path without requiring to fly over ground based facilities.
RNAV allows pilots to manage horizontal and vertical separation with other aircrafts without obtaining any ATC clearance.
RNAV allows to obtain ATC clairance by HF  radio without the requirement to establish any radio contact.
true: RNAV allows aircraft to take a more direct flight path without requiring to fly over ground based facilities.

question: Which of the following is one of the functions of the Computer in a basic RNAV system?
options: It automatically selects the two strongest transmitters for the Area-Nav-Mode and continues working by memory in case one of the two necessary station goes off the air
It checks the ground station accuracy using a built-in test programme
It transfers the information given by a VOR/DME station into tracking and distance indications to any chosen Phantom Station/waypoint
It calculates cross track information for NDB approaches
true: It transfers the information given by a VOR/DME station into tracking and distance indications to any chosen Phantom Station/waypoint

question: A phantom station (as used in a 2D RNAV-system) is:
options: A waypoint defined by two DME-distances from two different VOR/DME-stations.
A waypoint defined by a radial and a DME-distance from a VOR/DME-station.
An existing VOR/DME-station created in the memory of the Navigation Computer Unit of the RNAV-system.
A non-existing VOR/DME-station defined by two DME distances.
true: A waypoint defined by a radial and a DME-distance from a VOR/DME-station.

question: The navigational function of the horizontal situation indicator (HSI) in relation to area navigation systems is:
options: The indication of the cross track distance (XTK).
The indication of the track angle error (TKE).
The indication of the wind component.
true: The indication of the cross track distance (XTK).

question: In an FMS, how does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system obtain DME information?
options: The pilot tunes the closest VOR/DME stations within range on the VOR/DME Area navigation control panel.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system has its own VHF NAV tuner and the system itself tunes the DME stations providing the best angular position lines.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation System has its own VHF NAV tuner and it always tunes the DME stations closest to the aircraft position.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system uses whatever stations are tuned on the aircraft's normal VHF NAV selector.
true: The VOR/DME Area Navigation system has its own VHF NAV tuner and the system itself tunes the DME stations providing the best angular position lines.

question: In order to enter a phantom waypoint that is designated by a VOR/DME simple RNAV system, the VOR/DME:
options: Has to be positively identified by one of the pilots
Does not have to be in range when entered or used
Does not have to be in range when entered but must be when used
true: Does not have to be in range when entered but must be when used

question: Refer to image. Which of the distances indicated will be shown on a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation Equipment when using a 'Phantom Station' at position 'X'?
Erratic indications may be experienced when flying towards a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System 'Phantom Station':
options: When operating at low altitudes close to the limit of reception range from the reference station
When the Phantom Station is out of range
When in the cone of silence overhead the Phantom Station
Because, under adverse conditions (relative bearing to the Phantom Station other than 180°/360°) it takes the computer more time to calculate the necessary information
true: When operating at low altitudes close to the limit of reception range from the reference station

question: Which one of the following lists information given by a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System when tracking inbound to a phantom waypoint?
options: Aircraft position in latitude and longitude

question: Which of the following lists information required to input a waypoint or 'Phantom Station' into a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System?
options: Magnetic Track and distance from the aircraft to the waypoint or 'Phantom Station'
Radials from a minimum of two VORs to the waypoint or 'Phantom Station'
Magnetic Track and distance to a VOR/DME from the waypoint or 'Phantom Station'
Radial and distance from a VOR/DME to the waypoint or 'Phantom Station'
true: Radial and distance from a VOR/DME to the waypoint or 'Phantom Station'

question: Refer to image Which of the distances indicated will be shown on a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation Equipment when using a 'Phantom Station'?
A pilot is flying between two waypoints defined by suitably located VOR/DMEs. Equipped with a simple 2D RNAV system, this pilot:
options: Reads cross track error and the distance to go on CDI or HSI.
Must update any altitude change in RNAV system to have correct cross track error.
Reads VOR/DME bearing and distance on CDI or HSI to compute himself the cross track error.
Enters relative position between his aircraft and the VOR/DMEs on CDU to calculate the cross track error.
true: Reads cross track error and the distance to go on CDI or HSI.

question: How does the Electronic Flight Instrument System display of a B737-400 respond to  the failure of a VHF navigation (VOR) receiver?
options: It removes the associated magenta deviation bar and/or pointer from the display
The pointer flashes and a VOR 1 or 2 failure warning bar appears
The pointer rotates around the display and a VOR 1 or 2 failure warning bar appears
The deviation bar and/or pointer change colour to red and flash intermittently
true: It removes the associated magenta deviation bar and/or pointer from the display

question: Which component of the Electronic Flight Instrument System generates the visual displays on the EADI and EHSI?
What is cross track distance (XTK-distance) in an Area Navigation System?
options: Distance between air position and Great Circle Track between active waypoints.
Distance between air position and planned track.
Distance between actual position and Great Circle Track between active waypoints.
Distance between actual position and next waypoint.
true: Distance between actual position and Great Circle Track between active waypoints.

question: Which of the following combinations is likely to result in the most accurate Area Navigation (RNAV) fixes?
Which one of the following inputs to an Area Navigation System (R-NAV) comes from an external, not on-board, system?
Under which of the following circumstances does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system switch to Dead Reckoning mode?
options: The system is receiving information from one VOR and two DMEs
The system is receiving information from one VOR and one DME
The system is receiving information from only one VOR
The system is receiving information from the two DMEs
true: The system is receiving information from only one VOR

question: Under which of the following circumstances does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system switch to Dead Reckoning mode?
options: VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is not receiving information from the Air Data Computer.
When 'DR' is selected by the pilot.
VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is not receiving information from the aircraft compass system.
VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is receiving neither radial nor distance data information from VOR/DME stations.
true: VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is receiving neither radial nor distance data information from VOR/DME stations.

question: How does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system select the DME-stations to be used for positioning?
options: The VOR/DME Area Navigation System has its own NAV tuner and it always tunes the DME stations closest to the aircraft position.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system uses whatever stations are tuned on the aircraft's normal NAV selector.
The pilot tunes the closest VOR/DME stations within range on the VOR/DME Area navigation control panel.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system has its own NAV tuner and the system itself tunes the DME stations providing the most accurate position.
true: The VOR/DME Area Navigation system has its own NAV tuner and the system itself tunes the DME stations providing the most accurate position.

question: Apart from radials and distances from VOR/DME stations, what information is required by the VOR/DME Area Navigation computer in order to calculate the wind?
options: Heading from the aircraft compass system and true airspeed from the air data computer
Vertical speed from the air data computer
True airspeed from the air data computer
Heading from the aircraft compass system
true: Heading from the aircraft compass system and true airspeed from the air data computer

question: On what data is a VOR/DME Area Navigation system operating in the dead reckoning mode?
options: TAS from the Air Data Computer; heading from the aircraft compass; the last computed W/V.
Radial from one VOR; distances from two DMEs; TAS from the Air Data computer; heading from the aircraft compass.
Radial from one VOR; distances from two DMEs.
TAS from the Air Data Computer; heading from the aircraft compass.
true: TAS from the Air Data Computer; heading from the aircraft compass; the last computed W/V.

question: Output from which of the following combination of navigational sources provide enough information to the RNAV-equipment to calculate the wind vector?
options: Compass system and Inertial Reference System
Inertial Reference System and Global Positioning System
Inertial Reference System and Air Data Computer
Global Positioning System and Compass System
true: Inertial Reference System and Air Data Computer

question: A fix obtained by Rho-Rho navigation is based on information from two:

Which of the following data can be found in the navigation database?

1. SIDs and STARs
2. Radio aids
3. Optimum flight level
4. Company routes
5. Cruise speeds
In which of the following cases would ETOs and ETA at destination calculated by the Flight Management Computer (FMC) be correct?
options: When the actual winds match the forecast winds, and the actual cruising Mach number is equal to the FMC calculated Mach number
When the ETOs and ETA are based on the forecast winds calculated from the actual take-off time
When the FMC positions and GS are accurate
When the FMC computes each ETO and ETA using the correct GS
true: When the actual winds match the forecast winds, and the actual cruising Mach number is equal to the FMC calculated Mach number

question: Which of the following gives the best information about the progress of a flight between 2 en-route waypoints from a RNAV equipment?
In relation to Area Navigation Systems (RNAV), which of the following is an Air Data input?
On a modern commercial aircraft, the  FMS provides:
options: Lateral and vertical navigation, , and guidance and performance management.
A 3D area navigation and an air/ground datalink.
true: Lateral and vertical navigation, , and guidance and performance management.

question: The Flight Management Computer (FMC) position is:
options: The computed position based on a number of sources (IRS, Radio, ILS, GPS etc)
The same as that given on the No. 1 IRS
Another source of aircraft position; it is independent of other navigation sources (IRS, Radio, ILS, etc)
The actual position of the aircraft at any point in time
true: The computed position based on a number of sources (IRS, Radio, ILS, GPS etc)

question: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to flight plans is stored in the:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to STARs and SIDs is stored in the:
The Flight Management System (FMS) is organised in such a way that:
options: The navigation database of the FMC is valid for one year
The navigation database is "read only" to the pilot
The pilot is able to modify the navigation database in the FMC between two updates
The navigation datbase of the FMC is created by the pilot
true: The navigation database is "read only" to the pilot

question: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to waypoints is stored in the:

Which of the following are stored in the navigation database of the Flight Management System (FMS)?

1. Waypoints
2. Details of radio navigation aids
3. Optimum altitudes
4. Company routes
5. Landing reference speeds

The  FMS Navigation Database usually contains:

1. Airport reference data
2. ATC frequencies
3. Company routes
4. Navigation Aid frequencies The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
On modern passenger aircraft, the navigation data base usually contains:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to cruising speeds is stored in the:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to aircraft flight envelope computations is stored in the:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to V1, VR and V2 speeds is stored in the:
In an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) data relating primarily to navigation in the FMC is provided by:
options: Inertial Reference Systems -Navigation radios -Terrain Collision Alerting System
GPS - Aircraft Weather Radar - Navigation radios
Inertial Reference Systems -Aircraft Weather Radar -Navigation radios

question: The Control and Display Unit (CDU) on an FMS is:
options: The system used to update the navigation database.
Used on ground only to monitor the maintenance procedure.
Used by the crew to input data into FMC.
true: Used by the crew to input data into FMC.

question: IRS is a self-contained system because it:
options: has the ability to calculate the aircraft's position with an accuracy comparable to the GPS-position.
has a battery back-up which guarantees continued functioning in case of main power-failure.
has the ability to calculate  the aircraft's radio position without any reference to either man-made or natural information.
calculates the position of the aircraft without reference to externally generated signals.
true: calculates the position of the aircraft without reference to externally generated signals.

question: Kalman filtering is used within:
The IRS is a self-contained system because:
options: The system generates a warning in case of a failure.
The calculation of the position does not require any software.
It operates independently of navigational aids outside the aircraft.
It operates off its own power supply
true: It operates independently of navigational aids outside the aircraft.

question: Concerning FMC databases:
options: The navigation database may be customized for the specific airline operations.
Only the navigation database may be customized by the company's cost index strategy.
Company database and FMC databases can not be used at the same time.
Only the performance database may be customized by the company.
true: The navigation database may be customized for the specific airline operations.

question: What is true about the FMC databases:
options: The navigation database contains the company's cost index strategy.
The performance database contains aeronautical information for the planned area of operations of the aircraft.
The data can not be customized for the specific airline operations.
The data includes SIDs, STARS and runway approaches.
true: The data includes SIDs, STARS and runway approaches.

question: Under CS-25 colour code rules, features displayed in red on an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS), indicate:
options: Cautions and abnormal sources; engaged modes
Flight envelope and system limits; engaged modes
Warnings; flight envelope and system limits
true: Warnings; flight envelope and system limits

question: Under CS-25 colour code rules, features displayed in amber/yellow on an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS), indicate:
Under CS-25 general colour code rules, features displayed in green on an Electronic Flight Information System should indicate:
Under CS-25 colour code rules features displayed in cyan/blue, on an Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), indicate:
Under CS-25 colour code rules for Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), increasing intensity of precipitation are coloured in the order:

The following are lists of some of the screen modes of an Electronic Horizontal Situation Indicator (EHSI) on a B737-400 . Which list correctly shows the modes in which radar returns cannot be displayed.?
options: EXP VOR/ ILS, PLAN and MAP
FULL VOR/ILS, EXP VOR/ILS and PLAN
FULL NAV, FULL VOR/ILS and PLAN
true: FULL NAV, FULL VOR/ILS and PLAN

question: The track-line on the Electronic Horizontal Situation Indicator (EHSI) or Navigation Display of an Electronic Flight Instrument System:
options: Indicates to the pilot that a manually selected heading is being flown
Represents the track of the aircraft over the ground. When it co-incides with the desired track, wind influence is compensated for
Indicates that the pilot has made a manual track selection
Corresponds to the calculated IRS TH and is correct during turns
true: Represents the track of the aircraft over the ground. When it co-incides with the desired track, wind influence is compensated for

options: Refer to image Which of the figures depicts an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) display in MAP mode?

TACAN

question:
20° Left
20° Right

Refer to image. The 'O' followed by the letters 'KABC' indicate:

Refer to image. The diagram indicates that the aircraft is to the:
options: Left of the localiser and below the glidepath
Left of the localiser and above the glidepath
Right of the localiser and below the glidepath
Right of the localiser and above the glidepath
true: Right of the localiser and below the glidepath

question: The Flight Management System (FMS) is organised in such a way that the pilot can:
options: Modify the data in the database between two updates
Insert navigation data between two database updates
Read and write at any time in the database
true: Insert navigation data between two database updates

question: In what piece of FMS equipment will the pilot enter the waypoint information for the route?
In a 2D RNAV system you have entered the DME and VOR data for two  waypoints. What do you use to work out the cross track errors when en-route from one to the other?
options: The pilot is presented with VOR/DME information which must then be correlated to the pressure instruments to determine the effect of altitude
The pilot takes the VOR and DME information and computes it himself
The pilot will have to calculate the wind and apply it to the VOR/DME information
Use the automatically computed values on the CDI/HSI.
true: Use the automatically computed values on the CDI/HSI.

question: When entering and using a phantom waypoint in area navigation equipment, you:
options: You must be in range of the referenced station to enter or use it
The referenced station must be positively identified by at least 1 pilot
You don't need to be within range of the referenced station to enter the waypoint, but you must be to use it
Don't need to be in range of the referenced station to enter or use it
true: You don't need to be within range of the referenced station to enter the waypoint, but you must be to use it

question: Refer to image What is the selected course shown on the EFIS Navigation Display?
Refer to image What is the value of the selected course shown on the EFIS display?
Refer to image What is the aircraft’s position, as determined from the annex shown?
options: Right of selected course, DME distance increasing
Right of selected course, DME distance decreasing
Left of selected course, DME distance decreasing
Left of selected course, DME distance increasing
true: Left of selected course, DME distance decreasing

question: What is X-track error?
options: Distance between air position and the great dircle line between two active waypoints.
Distance between air position and planned track
Distance between actual position and the great circle line between two active waypoints
Distance between actual position and air position.
true: Distance between actual position and the great circle line between two active waypoints

question: Refer to the Annex to answer this question. Which of the following distances is shown on a VOR/DME based area navigation display using phantom waypoint X?
Which reference datum is used by the Russian Globalnaya Navigatsionnaya Sputnikovaya Sistema (GLONASS) satellite system?
An aircraft is flying at FL 150 with a modern EADI display.  Which of the following is not displayed?
What additional item of equipment is required if an FMS is to control vertical position.
When preparing your aircraft to commence a descent the ATS unit requests you to arrive at a level overhead the waypoint at a specified time.  Which RNAV system would be required for this?
With regard to the FMS navigational database, which of the following statements is correct?
options: The navigational database is write-protected and it is not possible to save crew-created navigational data.
Crew-inserted navigational data is deleted automatically after each flight.
The crew can overwrite existing data in the navigational database.
The navigational database is read-only, but space exists for crew-created navigational data.
true: The navigational database is read-only, but space exists for crew-created navigational data.

question: What FMS modes must be selected to enable it to automatically control both horizontal and vertical navigation?
Which of the following uses a Kalman filter?
Which FMS guidance modes does the pilot have to engage in order to follow the horizontal path, vertical path, and speed profile?
options: TRK (Track Navigation) and FLCH (Flight Level Change)
HDG (Heading Navigation) and VS/FPA (Vertical Speed/Flight Path Angle).

question: Which of the following navigation aids will provide an RNAV system with position?
Which of the following is NOT displayed on a modern EADI?
Fuel flow parameters are contained in...
Where is the information about wind direction and velocity which is displayed on the EFIS derived from?
options: Wind information is directly entered into the flight management computer (FMC) by pilots or dispatchers
the pilors enter the wind manually, based on currewnt in-flight information
Wind information is calculated by the Flight Management Computer (FMC) from heading, drift, true air speed and ground speed.
Wind information is updated by data link from the nearest met Office
true: Wind information is calculated by the Flight Management Computer (FMC) from heading, drift, true air speed and ground speed.

question: A pilot is flying at FL150 in an aircraft equipped with a modern EADI (Electronic Attitude Direction Indicator). One of the data listed below is not displayed on the cEADI in this phase of flight. It is...
